Production Plants to improve Engineering and Construction 
Agostino G. Bruzzone1,2,*, Kirill Sinelshchikov3, Marco Gotelli1,2, Filippo Monaci2, 
Xhulia Sina2, Filippo Ghisi2, Luca Cirillo2, Antonio Giovannetti2

Abstract 
Floating Production Storage and Offloading (FPSO) units are essential yet complex assets in offshore oil and gas production. Due 
to their significant scale and the intricate nature of their construction and conversion processes, FPSO projects frequently face 
delays and cost overruns. This paper presents a strategic engineering approach integrating risk management frameworks with 
machine learning to enhance project execution for FPSO units. By focusing on early Material Take Off (MTO) estimations, Critical 
Path analysis, and Local Content requirements, this approach addresses major challenges in FPSO projects, such as supply chain 
constraints and fluctuating market conditions for exotic materials. Our hybrid model combines historical data analysis with neural 
network-based process-specific correlations to improve MTO accuracy and procurement planning. Results demonstrate that this 
approach not only mitigates project risks but also enhances efficiency, enabling smoother workflows and minimizing delays. The 
findings offer actionable insights and tools for project managers aiming to optimize FPSO project delivery within the smart industry 
framework.

1. Introduction 
One of the most important innovations in offshore industry are the Floating Production Storage and Offloading units 
used for the extraction of oil and gas. These unit vessels are designed to receive oil and gas produced by the unit itself, 
or by nearby facilities (on water or subsea platforms), eliminating the need for expensive long-distance and often 
unfeasible pipelines from the underwater well to the onshore platform. There are 56 FPSO vessels expected to 
commence operations in 2022-2027, with Brazil being the main contributor with 22 FPSO additions (Statista 2024). 
However, these projects are affected by significant delays and cost overruns caused by complications in all the projects 
phases from the preliminary phase, engineering, procurement, construction and project handover [1][5]. The effective 
management of large-scale offshore projects presents a great challenge, especially when it comes to delay 
management, indeed it should be managed through a holistic and systematic approach [6]. For instance, the study of 
nine FPSO projects revealed that they had a combined overrun of 38% and collective projects delay of 12 years [9]. 
The construction sector is historically affected by low productivity, however FPSO construction or conversion is 
distinguished by substantial capital requirement with initial investments that typically vary from 2.0-3.0 billion USD 
[16]. Given these high financial stakes, the capacity to improve the delivery schedule for FPSO even by months or 
weeks can substantially influence the project’s overall profitability. In this paper the causes of time and cost overdue 
will be explored; there will also be proposed an integrated strategic approach to mitigate these problematics, followed 
by the description of a use case.  
1.1. Addressing root causes of project overdue 
Even though FPSO projects have brought numerous advantages in terms of flexibility and overcoming physical 
limitations, their complex nature is associated with risks and complications that challenge the projects delivery causing 
economic losses, disagreements and even project abandonment [19]. For systematic research and evaluation of these 
shortcomings, delay causes were divided into three categories: 
 Project Execution 
 Supply Chain Strategy 
 Construction Strategy 
 
The main contributors of the ineffective project management of FPSO projects arise from overengineering during 
the design phase, unexpected changes in the scope of conversions due to unforeseen conditions (the conversion of 
existing vessels into FPSO units require large modifications to meet specific operational needs), and unclear 
delineation of contractual responsibilities. Ineffective execution strategies are largely driven by local content 
constraints imposed by clients. The main clients of FPSO projects are PetroBras, ExxonMobil, Total Energies, etc, 
with high concentration on South America. As a matter of fact, according to surveys in 2021, these FPSOs operate in 
more than 30 countries across the globe where Brazil is the most active region. After Brazil, the most active regions 
for new orders are Guyana, Norway, India, and Senegal [13]. These countries impose local content requirements that 
impose a minimum threshold of goods and services, like the workforce, be purchased locally. While these 
requirements aim to benefit the local economy, they pose significant challenges to FPSO projects, considering the 
varying levels of capability and capacity of local yards and suppliers. These can lead to suboptimal performance, 
quality issues, and extensive rework, all of which contribute to project delays, for instance the Nigerian Egina project 
[14]. 
 
Another critical factor contributing to FPSO prolongated projects timeline is ineffective management of the 
Supply Chain. Supply Chain Strategy is often based on standard EPC (Engineering, Procurement and Construction) 
project execution model, that works typically well for traditional projects. However, FPSO projects with their 
complex nature are more vulnerable to late changes, as unanticipated requirements can impact procurement 
schedules significantly, delaying the project timeline [12]. Another critical aspect are the fluctuations to the global 
market conditions, that are reflected by variety in the oil and gas demand that is often linked to global oil price 
changes. These changes can lead to surges in demand for FPSO-related materials, creating bottlenecks, particularly

for critical components. This market saturation, along with volatile prices of raw materials like aluminium and steel, 
has led to procurement challenges that heighten project costs and delay timelines [11].   
 
To solve this problem in this study there is presented a strategic engineering model that exploits the technological 
innovations of recent years. This strategy provides for better control of changes during the projects and delay 
contemption introducing: 
- Systematic and consistent data acquisition creating a reliable and comparable data source. 
- Analysing historical data by identifying recurrent critical threats to the Critical Path.  
- Early estimation of MTO by introducing two previously studied method, for early Material 
Procurement.  
- Minimization of Local Content Penalties.  
 
This model extracts value from historical data by enhancing project execution efficiency and effectiveness in 
FPSO projects. 
2. Materials and Methods 
2.1. Systematic Data Acquisition 
The proposed Data Acquisition framework concentrates on systematically gathering data from pasts and current 
FPSO projects, that could give essential information for accurately constructing Material Take Off (MTO), 
Maintenance Activity Support and Data Mining to find process optimization opportunities. In maintenance, several 
methodologies and platforms are dedicated to data acquisition, supporting predictive and preventive strategies (like 
Acoustic Imaging for Leak detection, thermal imaging for electrical inspections) [4][10]. On the other hand, the MTO 
process that focuses on estimating procurement schedules and material quantity planning, need sufficient historical 
data for predictive methodologies. Current advancement of MTO process automation involves API (Application 
Programming Interface) codes that facilitate the BIM (Building Information modelling) employment in construction 
industry and allows the planner to define the work areas and estimate the required material/workhours for each area 
[17]. Even though this method proves to be effective in civil engineering projects, it presents problematics to FPSO 
projects that are subjected to customization to specific field requirements, harsh environmental and operational 
conditions and Dynamic Design Changes [3]. Automated MTO process must be adaptable to these changes. From 
thorough analysis of project schedules there was identified that the critical path in FPSO is largely influenced by the 
piping sector, as it will be further explained. Given this, particular attention was paid to data representing this sector 
considering at the same time other sectors that make up the project. A methodical approach is required to make sure 
for data accuracy, completeness, and standardization across all relevant parameters, thus systems engineering 
methodologies are applied to enhance data integration and management.  
The first component of this method is the construction of centralized databases and warehouses that consolidate 
information from various subsystems ensuring data consistency and accessibility.  Systematic research was conducted 
for the construction of a centralized database that contains data of several FPSO projects, distributed between several 
clients involving projects of construction and conversion. This process begins with defining and categorizing each 
data field based on project-specific needs, such as Project Number, Service Descriptions, Material Specifications, Unit 
of Measure, and Weight metrics. Once the database have been populated with values from relevant sources, including 
engineering design standards and material specifications relevant to the projects, all collected data undergoes a process 
of verification and analysis to check for errors, inconsistencies and missing values. This database constitute the 
foundation for all the subsequent analysis.

The development of this predictive neural network models based on high-granularity historical datasets helps 
project management in enhancing MTO estimation accuracy and procurement planning. The proactive strategy of 
early fabrication, supported by accurate material quantity estimation and optimized transportation logistics, 
contributes to smoother workflows and reduced risk of delays. Moreover, the establishment of standardized processes 
and clear collaboration protocols between Contractors and Yards fosters more efficient project execution. Future 
projects can benefit from these methodologies by ensuring thorough preparation and the integration of robust 
estimation and procurement frameworks from the project's start. This preparation will facilitate the effective 
implementation of early work strategies, thereby enhancing overall project efficiency and success. Where historical 
data is relevant, previous project databases should be referenced, ensuring that variables such as pipe size, thickness, 
and material (e.g., Carbon Steel or Super Duplex Stainless Steel) are consistent with current project specifications. 
 
2.4. Local Content Requirements  
Local Content requirements are policies that encourage the deploy of local resources like labor, materials, goods 
and services to avoid the ‘resource curse’, especially countries rich in oil. The objective of this study is to mitigate the 
risk of incurring local content penalties and to explore innovative pathways for reducing project costs. This 
investigation specifically examines the potential for minimizing local content fines by prioritizing the procurement of 
bulk materials locally in Brazil and subsequently transporting them to foreign construction yards [8]. This approach 
involves two key components: early procurement of materials (supported by the timely generation of Material Take-
Off, or MTO) and early construction activities. 
This study illustrates that strengthening the local supply chain for raw materials and shipping them to foreign yards 
where skilled labor and experienced manpower are available at competitive rates can offer significant cost advantages. 
Materials shipped out of Brazil benefit from a special tax-free regime, such as the Repetro regime, which exempts 
temporary exports of materials for projects abroad. In this study, foreign construction yards in Asia, particularly in 
China, are considered for their capacity and cost efficiency. 
As Brazil hosts the largest number of FPSO projects, its laws and regulations are used as reference and its local 
content was considered a 40 % work completed locally, with penalties imposed for non-compliance.  
Assuming that all structural materials are available simultaneously for transportation, the strategy effectively 
reduces delays and aligns material availability with project schedules. This integrated approach not only mitigates the 
risk of material shortages but also supports streamlined project execution through strategic resource mobilization and 
cost management. By prioritizing local procurement and optimizing transportation logistics, FPSO projects can 
achieve enhanced accuracy in material estimation, reduced penalties, and improved overall project outcomes. 
3. Results and Discussion  
This study highlights the effectiveness of applying the strategic engineering approach to address the unique 
challenges and risks associated with FPSO (Floating Production Storage and Offloading) project execution. By 
integrating this framework with Machine Learning techniques, Critical Path analysis, early Material Take Off 
estimation, and Local Content Strategies, we identified multiple benefits for the planning and execution phases of 
FPSO projects.  
By closely analyzing the project schedules, the research identified structural, piping, electrical and instrumentation 
areas as critical parts, where delays commonly occur, each with unique challenges and mitigation needs. Especially

Agostino G. Bruzzone  et al. / Procedia Computer Science 253 (2025) 3318–3324
the piping discipline faces significant delays due to the limited availability and procurement complexities of special 
materials that are vital for the FPSO project and that are extremely susceptible to external and environmental pressure 
like changes in market price or scarcity of raw materials. 
To address these challenges, this study explores early Material Take-Off (MTO) estimation as a proactive solution, 
utilizing a hybrid model that combines the efficiency of historical data analysis with the precision of a neural network 
model tailored to enhance process-specific correlations. This integrative model enables detailed, multi-level 
assessments of systems, and materials, especially critical for long-lead items like special steels.  
The findings of this study provide useful insights for enhancing FPSO project efficiency through improved MTO 
estimation, accurate procurement planning, and streamlined workflows. The proactive strategies developed, including 
early fabrication of critical components, enable smoother project execution and reduce the risk of schedule delays.
